serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of fried fish beehoon soup with milk is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of rice vermicelli, several pieces of deep-fried fish, vegetables, and a creamy milk-enriched soup broth.

nutrient_source.title: Fried fish beehoon soup (with milk) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Fried fish beehoon soup (with milk)', CR ID F05060059. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) GI — analogous estimate based on rice noodles/rice vermicelli entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for fried fish beehoon soup with milk as a composite dish. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component — rice vermicelli (beehoon) — which has a published GI of approximately 58 in the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles/rice vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (carbohydrate_g 9.44/100 × 450g serving = 42.5g) / 100 = 24.6 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 302 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1,359 mg sodium, representing over 59% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid drinking the soup broth entirely, request reduced fish stock and less added salt, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) is a refined high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~58) with low dietary fibre (0.88 g/100 g), which can elevate post-meal blood glucose; a full bowl delivers approximately 42.5 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller beehoon portion with extra fish and vegetables, avoid drinking all the broth, and pair this dish with a high-fibre side to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and contributes saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g); high sodium (302 mg/100 g) adds cardiovascular risk; however, fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request steamed or lightly fried fish where possible, avoid drinking the soup broth to limit sodium, and keep portion size moderate to manage saturated fat and total caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation; however, fish provides omega-3 fatty acids and evaporated milk contributes calcium supporting bone health in patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request lightly fried or steamed fish to reduce AGE exposure, avoid drinking all the broth to limit sodium, and be aware that tomato (a nightshade) may be a trigger for those with nightshade sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Beehoon Soup with Milk is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of deep-fried sliced fish pieces served in a milky, creamy soup broth made with fish stock and evaporated milk, accompanied by thick rice vermicelli (beehoon) and vegetables such as tomato, lettuce, and spring onion. The dish is characterised by its rich, savoury-sweet broth with a silky texture imparted by the evaporated milk, contrasting with the crispy exterior of the deep-fried fish. Per 100 g, the dish provides 73 kcal, 2.54 g protein, 3.42 g fat, 9.44 g carbohydrate, 0.88 g dietary fibre, and 302 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the rice vermicelli component. Clinically, the high sodium content (302 mg/100 g; ~1,359 mg per 450 g bowl) is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The refined beehoon base with low fibre presents a moderate glycaemic risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The fish component contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, and the evaporated milk adds calcium. This dish is widely available at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal.
